paragraphs.text: Jacob walked all day and lodged on the way. And when evening came, instead of the pillow he had used in his mother’s tent, “he took a stone, and put it for his pillows, and lay down to sleep… And he dreamed: and behold, a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven; and the angels of God ascended and descended on it: and the Lord stood above it” (Gen 28:11–13). In the image of the ladder there was shown to Jacob the ascending and descending of angels over him while he slept, and it was given to be understood that he was guarded not only in his waking but also when he slept; for then the angels were commanded to ascend and descend over him to guard him.

So, in the vision of the ladder God plainly showed Jacob the hidden providence over him. While he slept, it seemed to him that he was sleeping in a land far from God. But when he awoke and saw what care was being given him in the wilderness, he said: “As in the house of God I slept, and as before the gates of heaven I rested.” And to show Jacob that the angels were ascending and descending for his protection, God said to him: “Behold, I am with thee, and will keep thee… whithersoever thou goest, and will bring thee again into this land: for I will not leave thee, until I have done to thee that which I have spoken to thee.”

“And… Jacob said: Surely the Lord is in this place, and He keepeth me; but I knew it not” (Gen 28:15–16). The oil which Jacob poured upon the pillar was either taken from a neighboring village or brought with him. And the oil poured by Jacob upon the stone represented the mystery of Christ hidden within him, who was to come. “And Jacob called the name of that place Bethel” — that is, “house of God,” as it had also been called earlier. “And he made… a vow over the stone, saying: If the Lord God will be with me… and will give me bread… and raiment… and this stone… shall be God’s house: and of all that thou shalt give me I will surely give the tenth unto thee” (Gen 28:18–20, 22). And in the stone was represented the mystery of the Church, to which are paid the vows and offerings of all the peoples that have entered into her.
